turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan has our opinion this morning in case 15-1498, Sessions versus Dimaya.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Under this country's immigration law an alien convicted of certain kinds of crimes must be deported. Some of the crimes listed are quite specific, but some aren't. One says that an alien must be deported if he has committed a crime of violence as defined in Section 16 and if you then turn to Section 16 you find something called a residual clause which says that a crime of violence includes "any felony that by its nature involves a substantial risk that physical force against the person or property of another may be used in the course of committing the offense." So essentially an alien will be deported if he commits a crime involving a substantial risk that force will be used, and the basic question in this case is whether that description is too vague, whether anyone can really know what crimes that phrase includes and so what it means? James Dimaya has been a lawful resident of the United States since 1992, but in that time he was convicted twice of first-degree burglary under California law. After the second offense the government sought to deport him. Its argument was that the California burglary was a crime of violence under Section 16 because it involved a substantial risk of force. While Dimaya's case was working its way through the system, this Court decided a case called Johnson v. United States. The statute at issue there called ACCA imposes higher sentences on people who have prior convictions for certain kinds of crimes. ACCA also had a so-called residual clause and it was quite similar to the one in Section 16. It said that a violent felony was a felony that involved conduct that presents a serious potential risk of physical injury to another. In Johnson we held that ACCA's residual clause was unconstitutionally vague, that it didn't provide fair notice of what crimes would lead to enhanced penalties. In considering Dimaya's case the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit held that our Johnson decision meant that Section 16 was also unconstitutionally vague. Today we uphold that decision. The same two features that made ACCA's residual clause too vague also played Section 16's. First, both residual clauses required to use of what we've called the ordinary case approach. Under that approach, courts don't look at a crime's elements or the defendant's specific conduct; instead, courts have to examine whether the ordinary case of an offense poses the requisite risk. But this meant judges had to speculate about what the ordinary case of any crime was, without any real guidance. Thus the ordinary case of burglary involve breaking into an occupied home at night or an unoccupied home by day, thus the ordinary case of witness tampering involve threatening to break someone's knees or instead just offering a bribe. A judge we said in Johnson, and we say again today, has no way to tell. Compounding that uncertainty both residual clauses use a fuzzy risk standard, in other words in saying substantial risks or potential serious risk the law is left murky just how much risk it took to make a crime a violent felony. The combination of those two things meant that it was too hard to tell what would count as a violent felony under either statute, and our Constitution insists that a person can't be left in the dark about what the law means. The government in this Court identified three textual differences between the two residual clauses which it said made ACCAs harder to understand and apply and Section 16 is easier. For reasons explained in our opinion, we think all three are distinctions without a difference. We hold that our decision in Johnson controls this case and we affirm the Ninth Circuit. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in Part 1, 3, 4B and 5 of the opinion and concurring in the judgment. The Chief Justice has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Kennedy, Thomas and Alito joined. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Kennedy and Alito joined as to Parts 1(C)2, 2(A)1 and 2B.
